headline: Ocean's Hidden Pantry: How Deep-Sea Pressure Feeds Marine Life
meta_description: Scientists discover that extreme ocean pressure releases nutrients from sinking particles, feeding deep-sea microbes and reshaping our understanding of Earth's…
summary_short: Researchers find that crushing deep-ocean pressure squeezes nutrients from sinking organic matter, providing unexpected food for microbes living miles below the surface.
body:
For decades, scientists assumed deep-sea microbes survived in a nutrient-starved world. New research from the University of Southern Denmark overturns that assumption entirely.

When tiny clumps of dead algae and organic material—known as marine snow—sink through the ocean, something unexpected happens around 2 to 6 kilometers depth. The immense hydrostatic pressure acts like a "giant juicer," forcing dissolved carbon and nitrogen out of the particles. Microbes living in the surrounding water immediately consume these leaked nutrients.

The numbers are striking: sinking marine snow loses up to 50% of its carbon and between 58% and 63% of its nitrogen during descent. In laboratory experiments using diatoms, bacterial abundance surged 30-fold within just two days after exposure to this released material.

The implications extend far beyond feeding deep-sea bacteria. This discovery could fundamentally reshape how scientists understand Earth's carbon cycle. If substantial carbon escapes these sinking particles before reaching the seafloor, less carbon gets permanently buried in ocean sediments than previously believed. Instead, that carbon remains suspended in deep waters for centuries or millennia before gradually rising back to the surface.

Next, researchers plan Arctic Ocean expeditions aboard the research vessel Polarstern to confirm this pressure-driven mechanism occurs throughout the world's oceans. The study was published in Science Advances.
